content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
April 22,
1994
Joseph Remcho
Remcho, Johansen and Purcell
220 Montgomery Street, Suite 800
San Francisco,
CA
94104
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—94-062
Dear Mr.
Remcho:
This is in reply to your request for advice on behalf of As
semblywoman Jackie Speier concerning the gift limitations of the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
QUESTIONS
1.
Assemblywoman Speier has established a trust fund for her
children, the Speier-Sierra Minor Children Trust Fund.
Some gifts
in excess of the current $270 gift limit have been made to the
trust fund.
On the assumption that the trustee of the fund is
someone other than Assemblywoman Speier and that the funds are to
be made available only to the children after they reach the age of
18,
may the trust fund for the children retain those gifts and
accept others?
2.
May the gifts be accepted if the trust provides that
funds may be used for the benefit of the children prior to their
turning age 18?
3.
May close family friends make a gift in excess of the
limit to Assemblywoman Speier or her children?
4.
May close family friends make continuing gifts over the
years to the children in excess of the gift limit?
5.
A group of tennis friends of Dr. Sierra’s plans a charity
tennis tournament and auction fundraiser for Assemblywoman Speier
and her children.
Goods would be donated to the auction and the
proceeds of the auction would go to the Speier-Sierra family.
The
fair market value of donated goods would come from the donor and
the difference (if any)
between that value and the amount paid at
1
Government Code Sections 81000-9IO15.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2, Divi
sion 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—94—062
Page
2
auction would come from the auction purchaser.
Must the value of
the donated goods above the fair market value and the amount
(over
fair market value) paid to purchase the item be limited to $270 or
may the family consider them gifts, to the children?
Do any limits
apply if all of the funds go to the Speier-Sierra Minor Children
Trust?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
Funds paid into the trust fund do not constitute a
reportable gift to Assemblywoman Speier if the funds are made
available to her children after they have attained the age of 18
and she has no discretion and control over the funds.
2.
Funds paid into the trust fund constitute a reportable
gift to Assemblywoman Speier if:
(1)
the funds are used to defray
the normal expenses of raising her children before they reach the
age of 18, or
(2) the funds otherwise come within her discretion
and control.
The gift is made when the funds are actually paid
from the trust for the children’s support or to Assemblywoman
Speier.
The source of the gift is not the trust fund but the
specific trust fund donor or donors to whom the payment from the
trust is attributed.
The trust fund would be the intermediary for
the gift.
3.
and 4.
Close family friends may not make gifts, in excess
of the gift limits for the purpose of lending financial assistance
to Assemblywoman Speier and her immediate family because of the
hardships brought about by Dr. Sierra’s death.
Such friends may,
however,
make annual gifts up to the gift limit for tIese
purposes.
5.
Funds provided from the tennis fundraiser/auction
constitute a reportable gift to Assemblywoman Speier if the funds
are used to defray her normal expenses for supporting her children
when they have not attained the age of
18 or if ‘the funds
otherwise come within, her discretion and control.
In this case,
the gift is made when the funds are actually paid either for the
children’s support or to Assemblywoman Speier.
See the Analysis
below for discussion of the sources of the gift and valuation.
The group organizing the event would be the intermediary for the
gift.
FACTS
Assemblywoman Speier’s husband,
Dr.
Steven Sierra, recently
was killed in an automobile accident.
Assemblywoman Speier and
Dr. Sierra have’ a son,
age five,
and Assemblywoman Speier expects
a second child in August.
Dr.. Sierra left substantial liabilities and no significant
life insurance.
The motorist responsible’for Dr. Sierra’s ac—
cidént was apparently uninsured.
There has been an outpouring of support for Assemblywoman
Speier and her family.
A number of people have contributed to a


File No.
A—94—062
Page
3
trust fund that was established for Assemblywoman Speier’s
children.
In addition, several long time family friends of As
seiublywoman Speier and Dr. Sierra desire to make gifts directly to
Assemblywoman Speier and the children.
For example,
long time
friends of Dr. Sierra who live in London desire to give the family
approximately $10,000 for necessary living expenses.
ANALYSIS
The Act regulates gifts to legislators in four ways.
Gifts
of $250 or more may create a conflict of interest for the legisla
tor and require herto disqualify herself when participating in
governmental decisions which financially affect the donor of the
gift.
(Sections 87100—87103.)
Gifts from individual lobbyists or
lobbying firms may not exceed $10 in a calendar month.
(Sections
86203 and 86204.)
Gifts totalling $50 or more from a single
source in a calendar year must be reported on a legislator’s
statement of economic interests.
(Section 87200.)
Finally, gifts
from a single source cannot exceed $270 in a calendar year unless
an exception to the gift limits applies.
2
(Section 89504.)
Trust Fund
Your first two questions concern the establishment of a trust
fund to be administered by a third party trustee for the benefit
of Assemblywoman Speier’s children.
Donations,
some in excess of
the $270 annual gift limit, are to be made to the fund and the
proceeds periodically paid to or on behalf of the children.
The
question is whether these donations would be considered gifts to
Assemblywoman Speier.
Regulation l8944 sets forth a test for determining whether a
gift to a family member is in fact a gift to the official.
Under
the regulation,
a gift given directly to an official’s family
member is not a gift to the official unless it is used or disposed
of by the official.
(Regulation 18944(a).)
Furthermore,
a gift
given to the official for a family member is not a gift to the
official unless the official exercises discretion and control over
the gift’s use.
(Regulation 18944(c).)
In any event,
when an
official enjoys a direct benefit from the gift, it is deemed to be
a gift in full to the official.
(Regulation 18944(d).)
Thus, under the regulation, if Assemblywoman Speier uses,
disposes of or exercises discretion and control over the trust
funds,
she will have received a gift of those funds.
Furthermore,
even if she has no control over the funds, but they are paid from
the trust and defray the normal expenses associated with support
ing the children,
she will have received a direct benefit from the
2
The gift limitation amount is revised in each odd-numbered
year, based upon changes in the Consumer Price Index.
(Section
89504; Regulation 18954.)
Regulation 18944,
formerly 18726.2,
was renumbered by the Com
mission on November 5,
1993.


File No.
A—94—062
Page
4
children’s gift and thus a reportable gift.
This will remain the
case so long as the children are her dependents.
We have defined
“dependent child” in previous advice to mean a natural or adopted
child who is under the age of
18 and eligible to be claimed as a
dependent for tax purposes.
(Staff Memorandum,
No.
78-151.)
Assuming payments from the trust fund are gifts to Assembly
woman Speier, the remaining issue is who would be the source of
the gift from the trust.
Regulation 18945(a) (1)
states that when
a payment
4 is made to a third party and the third party in fact
directs and controls use of the payment to make a gift to an of
ficial, the third party is the source of the gift.
However, where
a donor makes a payment to a third party and earmarks the payment
for a specific official, the donor is directing and controlling
the making of the gift and is thus the maker of the gift.
In the circumstances you describe concerning this proposed
trust,
Assemblywoman Speier is the earmarked recipient for any
trust funds that will be used to pay the normal expenses of sup
porting her dependent children.
Accordingly, the source of the
gift to Assemblywoman Speier would be each donor to the trust and
not the trust itself,
and the gift is made to Assemblywoman Speier
when the funds are paid from the trust.
The trust fund would be
reported as an intermediary pursuant to Section 87210.
To ascertain when a particular trust donor is making a gift
to Assemblywoman Speier, the trustee should keep records so that
each gift is appropriately attributed when made.
This will assist
Assemblywoman Speier in reporting these gifts.
Furthermore,
we
direct your attention to Section 86112.5,
which requires
lobbyists,
lobbying firms and lobbyist employers to provide
notification of gifts made.
Therefore,
Assemblywoman Speier may
receive up to $270 per donor for each year she remains subject to
the gift limitations of Section 89504.6
Close Family Friends
Your third question asks whether,
in Assemblywoman Speier’s
unique circumstances, close family friends may make gifts to her
in excess of the gift limits in Section 89504.
If the answer to
that question is in the affirmative,
you ask if such gifts may
continue over more than one year.
You believe that Section
89504(b) (2)
permits this.
Section 82044 defines “payment” as a “...
payment, distribu
tion, transfer,
loan,
advance, deposit, gift or other rendering of
money, property, services or anything else of
value....”
-
Subject to revisions to the gift limitation amount.
See
footnote 2,
supra.
6
Please note that lobbyists and lobbying firms are subject to a
$10 per month gift limit under Section 86203.


File No.
A—94—062
Page
5
Section 89504(a) establishes the gift limit.
Section
89504(b) sets forth exceptions to the gift limit.
Specifically,
Section 89504(b) (2) states:
This section shall not prohibit or limit the following:
***
(2)
Wedding gifts and gifts exchanged btween
individuals on birthdays, holidays and other
similar occasions, provided that the gifts
exchanged are not substantially disproportion—
-
ate in value.
7
(Emphasis added.)
The exception in Section 89504 (b) (2) has its roots in the
Commission’s Cory Opinion
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
153.
In Cory, the
Commission ruled that volunteer personal services rendered by a
neighbor to an official were not gifts within the meaning of the
Act.
The Commission based its ruling upon the Act’s purposes for
requiring officials to disclose financial information, to wit:
Assets and income of public officials which may be
materially affected by their official actions should be
disclosed
(Section 81002(c).)
The Commission reasoned that acts of neighborliness, while
conferring a theoretical economic benefit on an official,
do not
represent an economià transaction which will subjectively bias the
official.
If any matter comes before the [official]
in which his
neighbor is interested,
[the official)
may or may not be
subjectively biased as a result of his like
(or dislike)
for the neighbor, but certainly no bias will be caused
by [the official’s) fear that his “income” will be
materially affected.
(Cory, at p.. 155.)
In 1978,
based upon the logic in Cory, the Commission adopted
Regulation 18727.
In subdivision
(b)
of this regulation, the Com
mission exempted gifts exchanged between a public official and an
-individual on holidays, birthdays, or similar such occasions,
provided the value of the official’s gift did not exceed the
other.
The basis for the exemption was stated in the staff
We note that Commission Regulation 18942 (a) (8)
exempts
“presents” given in similar circumstances’from being gifts for
purposes of reporting or gift limits.
We read the term “presents”
to be narrower than the term “gifts” set forth in Section
89504(b) (2).
Therefore,
we do not apply Regulation 18942 (a) (8) ‘S
exception to cash gifts of more than a nominal Value.


File No.
A—94—062
Page
6
memorandum describing the regulation to the Commission which
stated,
in pertinent part:
We believe that with respect to [the exemption],
considerations similar to those expressed by the Cominis—
sion in the
...
Cory Opinion
... apply.
In other words,
although an official might be biased by his or her
friendship with the person with whom the official
routinely exchanges gifts, the bias does not arise from
the monetary value of the gifts, and such gifts are
therefore outside the policy considerations, of the Act.
(Memorandum to Commission
regarding Home Hospitality and
Other Gifts Which Must be
Reported by Officials
- Regula
tions 18630 and 18727, April
24,
1978.)8
Section 89504 was passed by the Legislature in 1990 (Stats.,
1990,
Ch.
84; Stats.
1990,
Ch.
1075).
According to its legisla—
tive history, the gift exchange exception in Section 89504(b) (2)
was based directly upon Commission Regulation 18727(b).
(See As
sembly Floor Analysis, Proposed Conference Report No.
1, April 5,
1990,
SB 1738,
p.. 3; Senate Floor Analysis,
Proposed Conference
Report No. .1, April 5,
1990,
SB 1738, p.4.)
Therefore, in
interpreting Section 89504(b) (2),
we may rely upon the
Commission’s logic in adopting the gift exchange exception in
Regulation 18727(b).
At the basis of the exception is the idea that gifts of
substantially equal value exchanged between an official-and a
close friend are unlikely to bias the official in the performance
of his or her duties.
Accordingly, these types of transactions
would logically fall outside the purview of the Act.
However,’ this exception is problematic as applied in As
semblywoman Speier’s situation because it is difficult to define
when a friend is so “close” to an official that financial
considerations hold no sway.
Based upon Regulation 18727(b),’ Sec
tion 89504(b) (2)
approaches the problem by essentially stating
that a friend with whom the official routinely exchanges gifts of
roughly the same value on special occasions is sufficiently’
“close” to merit the exception.
This approach is not adaptable to Assemblywoman Speier’s
situation.
Deaths of family members often occur infrequently at
great intervals, making it impossible to determine if subsequent
gifts to the deceased’s family would be routinely reciprocated and
be of substantially the same value.
Or—course, if Assemblywoman
Speier and Dr. Sierra have in the past provided this type Of
assistance to someone and that person now’wishes to reciprocate by
8
In 1994, Regulation 18727(b)
was revised and placed in Commis
sion Regulation 18942 (a) (8).
See footnote 6,
supra.


File No.
A—94—062
Page 7
providing assistance of roughly the same value, the exception
would apply.
However, it is too speculative to assume that
Assemblywoman Speier will provide such reciprocal assistance in
the, future in exchange for financial assistance she receives at
this time.
Accordingly,
we cannot apply Section 89504(b)(2)’s exception
in these circumstances.
Auction Fundraiser
Your final question is whether funds raised at an auction of
goods which are donated and auctioned off for the purpose of
providing funds to Assemblywoman Speier’s children are gifts to
the children and not Assemblywoman Speier.
If these are
considered to be gifts to the Assemblywoman,
you ask if it would
make a difference if the funds were deposited into the children’s
trust fund discussed above.
We view the goods donated to and funds raised at the auction
to be similar to the funds donated to the trust fund discussed
above.
In other words, if Assemblywoman Speier uses, disposes of
or exercises discretion and control over the funds, they will be
gifts to her.
(Regulation 18944(a),(c).)
In addition,
even if
she has no control over the funds but they are paid to defray her
normal expenses for supporting the children while they are
dependents and under 18 years of age,
she will have received a
direct benefit and thus a gift in the full amount of the moneys
provided.
(Regulation 18944(d).)
Under this analysis, it
therefore makes no difference whether the funds are provided
directly from the organizers of the auction or through the
children’s trust.
Since the proceeds of the auction are earmarked for Assembly
woman Speier or her children, the sources of the gifts would be
the individual donors and purchasers and not the group which
organized the event.
The group organizing the event would be
reported as an intermediary pursuant to Section 87210.
(Regulation 18945(a)(1).)
However, the gift would not be made to
Assemblywoman Speier until she comes into control of the funds or
when they are paid for the support of her children.
These
individuals’ donations would be valued as follows:
For donors of
goods, the fair market value of the goods.
For purchasers of
goods, the value paid less the fair market value.
(Example:
John
Doe donates one free night at his condominiu!n at Lake Tahoe, which
has a fair market rental value of $200.
Jane Roe purchases the
free night in the condominium for $300.
Assuming all of the $300
is provided in the same calendar year to Assemblywoman Speier or
her children,
John Doe has made a $200 gift and Jane Roe has made
a $100 gift.)
Therefore,
Assemblywoman- Speier may receive up to
$270 per donor for each year sheremains subject to the gift
limitations of Section 89504.
Finally,
as described above in
regards to the trust fund, the organizers of the auction should
See footnotes 2,
5 and
6 supra.


File No.
A—94—062
Page 8
keep records so that each gift is appropriately attributed when
made.
-
Should you have additional questions, please contact me at
916/
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General/Counsel
B
.
Scott Ha labrin
Assistant General Counsel
SGC:SH:ak

limit to Assemblywoman Speier or her children?

Goods would be donated to the auction and the

auction would come from the auction purchaser.

sections.conclusion:
1.
Funds paid into the trust fund do not constitute a
reportable gift to Assemblywoman Speier if the funds are made
available to her children after they have attained the age of 18
and she has no discretion and control over the funds.
2.
Funds paid into the trust fund constitute a reportable
gift to Assemblywoman Speier if:
(1)
the funds are used to defray
the normal expenses of raising her children before they reach the
age of 18, or
(2) the funds otherwise come within her discretion
and control.
The gift is made when the funds are actually paid
from the trust for the children’s support or to Assemblywoman
Speier.
The source of the gift is not the trust fund but the
specific trust fund donor or donors to whom the payment from the
trust is attributed.
The trust fund would be the intermediary for
the gift.
3.
and 4.
Close family friends may not make gifts, in excess
of the gift limits for the purpose of lending financial assistance
to Assemblywoman Speier and her immediate family because of the
hardships brought about by Dr. Sierra’s death.
Such friends may,
however,
make annual gifts up to the gift limit for tIese
purposes.
5.
Funds provided from the tennis fundraiser/auction
constitute a reportable gift to Assemblywoman Speier if the funds
are used to defray her normal expenses for supporting her children
when they have not attained the age of
18 or if ‘the funds
otherwise come within, her discretion and control.
In this case,
the gift is made when the funds are actually paid either for the
children’s support or to Assemblywoman Speier.
See the Analysis
below for discussion of the sources of the gift and valuation.
The group organizing the event would be the intermediary for the
gift.

sections.analysis:
The Act regulates gifts to legislators in four ways.
Gifts
of $250 or more may create a conflict of interest for the legisla
tor and require herto disqualify herself when participating in
governmental decisions which financially affect the donor of the
gift.
(Sections 87100—87103.)
Gifts from individual lobbyists or
lobbying firms may not exceed $10 in a calendar month.
(Sections
86203 and 86204.)
Gifts totalling $50 or more from a single
source in a calendar year must be reported on a legislator’s
statement of economic interests.
(Section 87200.)
Finally, gifts
from a single source cannot exceed $270 in a calendar year unless
an exception to the gift limits applies.
2
(Section 89504.)
Trust Fund
Your first two questions concern the establishment of a trust
fund to be administered by a third party trustee for the benefit
of Assemblywoman Speier’s children.
Donations,
some in excess of
the $270 annual gift limit, are to be made to the fund and the
proceeds periodically paid to or on behalf of the children.
The
question is whether these donations would be considered gifts to
Assemblywoman Speier.
Regulation l8944 sets forth a test for determining whether a
gift to a family member is in fact a gift to the official.
Under
the regulation,
a gift given directly to an official’s family
member is not a gift to the official unless it is used or disposed
of by the official.
(Regulation 18944(a).)
Furthermore,
a gift
given to the official for a family member is not a gift to the
official unless the official exercises discretion and control over
the gift’s use.
(Regulation 18944(c).)
In any event,
when an
official enjoys a direct benefit from the gift, it is deemed to be
a gift in full to the official.
(Regulation 18944(d).)
Thus, under the regulation, if Assemblywoman Speier uses,
disposes of or exercises discretion and control over the trust
funds,
she will have received a gift of those funds.
Furthermore,
even if she has no control over the funds, but they are paid from
the trust and defray the normal expenses associated with support
ing the children,
she will have received a direct benefit from the
2
The gift limitation amount is revised in each odd-numbered
year, based upon changes in the Consumer Price Index.
(Section
89504; Regulation 18954.)
Regulation 18944,
formerly 18726.2,
was renumbered by the Com
mission on November 5,
1993.


File No.
A—94—062
children’s gift and thus a reportable gift.
This will remain the
case so long as the children are her dependents.
We have defined
“dependent child” in previous advice to mean a natural or adopted
child who is under the age of
18 and eligible to be claimed as a
dependent for tax purposes.
(Staff Memorandum,
No.
78-151.)
Assuming payments from the trust fund are gifts to Assembly
woman Speier, the remaining issue is who would be the source of
the gift from the trust.
Regulation 18945(a) (1)
states that when
a payment
4 is made to a third party and the third party in fact
directs and controls use of the payment to make a gift to an of
ficial, the third party is the source of the gift.
However, where
a donor makes a payment to a third party and earmarks the payment
for a specific official, the donor is directing and controlling
the making of the gift and is thus the maker of the gift.
In the circumstances you describe concerning this proposed
trust,
Assemblywoman Speier is the earmarked recipient for any
trust funds that will be used to pay the normal expenses of sup
porting her dependent children.
Accordingly, the source of the
gift to Assemblywoman Speier would be each donor to the trust and
not the trust itself,
and the gift is made to Assemblywoman Speier
when the funds are paid from the trust.
The trust fund would be
reported as an intermediary pursuant to Section 87210.
To ascertain when a particular trust donor is making a gift
to Assemblywoman Speier, the trustee should keep records so that
each gift is appropriately attributed when made.
This will assist
Assemblywoman Speier in reporting these gifts.
Furthermore,
we
direct your attention to Section 86112.5,
which requires
lobbyists,
lobbying firms and lobbyist employers to provide
notification of gifts made.
Therefore,
Assemblywoman Speier may
receive up to $270 per donor for each year she remains subject to
the gift limitations of Section 89504.6

you ask if such gifts may

You believe that Section

permits this.

loan,

value....”

Assemblywoman Speier has established a trust fund for her
children, the Speier-Sierra Minor Children Trust Fund.
Some gifts
in excess of the current $270 gift limit have been made to the
trust fund.
On the assumption that the trustee of the fund is
someone other than Assemblywoman Speier and that the funds are to

18,
may the trust fund for the children retain those gifts and
accept others?

funds may be used for the benefit of the children prior to their
turning age 18?

limit to Assemblywoman Speier or her children?

years to the children in excess of the gift limit?

and her children.
Goods would be donated to the auction and the

18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2, Divi
sion 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

auction would come from the auction purchaser.
Must the value of
the donated goods above the fair market value and the amount
(over
fair market value) paid to purchase the item be limited to $270 or
may the family consider them gifts, to the children?
Do any limits
apply if all of the funds go to the Speier-Sierra Minor Children
Trust?

CONCLUSION: 1.
Funds paid into the trust fund do not constitute a
reportable gift to Assemblywoman Speier if the funds are made
available to her children after they have attained the age of 18
and she has no discretion and control over the funds.
2.
Funds paid into the trust fund constitute a reportable
gift to Assemblywoman Speier if:
(1)
the funds are used to defray
the normal expenses of raising her children before they reach the
age of 18, or
(2) the funds otherwise come within her discretion
and control.
The gift is made when the funds are actually paid
from the trust for the children’s support or to Assemblywoman
Speier.
The source of the gift is not the trust fund but the
specific trust fund donor or donors to whom the payment from the
trust is attributed.
The trust fund would be the intermediary for
the gift.
3.
and 4.
Close family friends may not make gifts, in excess
of the gift limits for the purpose of lending financial assistance
to Assemblywoman Speier and her immediate family because of the
hardships brought about by Dr. Sierra’s death.
Such friends may,
however,
make annual gifts up to the gift limit for tIese
purposes.
5.
Funds provided from the tennis fundraiser/auction
constitute a reportable gift to Assemblywoman Speier if the funds
are used to defray her normal expenses for supporting her children
when they have not attained the age of
18 or if ‘the funds
otherwise come within, her discretion and control.
In this case,
the gift is made when the funds are actually paid either for the
children’s support or to Assemblywoman Speier.
See the Analysis
below for discussion of the sources of the gift and valuation.
The group organizing the event would be the intermediary for the
gift.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission April 22, 1994 Joseph Remcho Remcho, Johansen and Purcell 220 Montgomery Street, Suite 800 San Francisco, CA 94104 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A—94-062 Dear Mr. Remcho: This is in reply to your request for advice on behalf of As semblywoman Jackie Speier concerning the gift limitations of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 QUESTIONS 1. Assemblywoman Speier has established a trust fund for her children, the Speier-Sierra Minor Children Trust Fund. Some gifts in excess of the current $270 gift limit have been made to the trust fund. On the assumption that the trustee of the fund is someone other than Assemblywoman Speier and that the funds are to be made available only to the children after they reach the age of 18, may the trust fund for the children retain those gifts and accept others? 2. May the gifts be accepted if the trust provides that funds may be used for the benefit of the children prior to their turning age 18? 3. May close family friends make a gift in excess of the limit to Assemblywoman Speier or her children? 4. May close family friends make continuing gifts over the years to the children in excess of the gift limit? 5. A group of tennis friends of Dr. Sierra’s plans a charity tennis tournament and auction fundraiser for Assemblywoman Speier and her children. Goods would be donated to the auction and the proceeds of the auction would go to the Speier-Sierra family. The fair market value of donated goods would come from the donor and the difference (if any) between that value and the amount paid at 1 Government Code Sections 81000-9IO15. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000—18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Divi sion 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 File No. A—94—062 Page 2 auction would come from the auction purchaser. Must the value of the donated goods above the fair market value and the amount (over fair market value) paid to purchase the item be limited to $270 or may the family consider them gifts, to the children? Do any limits apply if all of the funds go to the Speier-Sierra Minor Children Trust? CONCLUSIONS 1. Funds paid into the trust fund do not constitute a reportable gift to Assemblywoman Speier if the funds are made available to her children after they have attained the age of 18 and she has no discretion and control over the funds. 2. Funds paid into the trust fund constitute a reportable gift to Assemblywoman Speier if: (1) the funds are used to defray the normal expenses of raising her children before they reach the age of 18, or (2) the funds otherwise come within her discretion and control.
